What do a typewriter and AI have in common? 🤖

Thoughts after visiting Anthropic’s San Francisco office last week:

The speed at which AI is advancing is staggering—and Germany is falling behind.

My visit to #Anthropic last week was eye-opening. They’ve doubled their workforce in a year and are hiring 150 people per week. Anthropic has a $380billion enterprise evaluation and just raised $30billion in a series G funding round. Since the recent policy shift around US government contracts, #Claude has hit record user #growth—reports suggest up to 1mio. new users daily.

For #software development and #programming, this is transformative. The way we code is fundamentally changing. Software is becoming a commodity.

Just this week, Anthropic released its newest and most powerful Claude #AI model yet - Claude #Mythos- capable of solving problems faster and more securely than ever (restricted release to partners only so far apparently). #Cybersecurity will never be the same, and we need to prepare.

So what does this mean for traditional German manufacturers?

We need to adapt fast. Companies must embrace AI-first thinking and build #agentic #workflows into their organization. This is happening now—not in five years.

And the typewriter? 🎹

At Anthropic, they had this beautiful installation: a vintage typewriter where you could write a prompt, feed it to Claude, and watch the AI’s response print out. #Innovation meeting #tradition. It perfectly captures the moment we’re in.

The real question for Europe: Are we moving fast enough?

What’s your take? How are businesses in your region adapting to the AI revolution?

The real AI conversation has shifted - from possibility to implementation, from hype to judgment, and from technology alone to people as well.

We didn't talk about the "Why" of AI. We didn’t need to. At the WomenExecs on Boards #APACSummit this week, I grounded my panel with Tan Su Shan (CEO, DBS Bank) and Anu Madgavkar (Partner, McKinsey Global Institute) firmly in the reality of implementation.

We stayed in the weeds, then zoomed out to the macro, exploring the uncomfortable spaces opening up between #macro_necessity and #micro_reality.

1. The Practitioner's Reality: Implementation as the Path 
Su Shan was remarkably candid about the friction of moving from "experimenting" to "production." She described a world where transformation isn't a smooth line - it involves starts, halts, and course corrections. This isn’t a sign of failure; it’s a sign that something real is being attempted. The core challenge for a Board is bridging the "VELOCITY MISMATCH": if technology moves in weekly sprints and Governance moves in 90-day cycles, the Board becomes a bottleneck, not a steward.

2. The Macro-Economic Mandate & "Unassociated Risks" Anu zoomed us out to the stark demographic reality: with a shrinking global workforce, AI is no longer a luxury-it is a fundamental requirement for productivity. But she also issued a warning about UNASSOCIATED RISKS-those second-order systemic effects, like shifts in social contracts and labor market stability, that boards often leave unexamined while focusing narrowly on project-level ROI.

3. The Structural Shift: The "Diamond-Shaped" Organization This tension is hollowing out the entry-level-the traditional base of the organizational pyramid. We are seeing the emergence of a "Diamond" structure: fat in the middle with experienced practitioners, but thin at the bottom. For a Board, this is a massive TALENT PIPELINE RISK. How do we build the leaders of 2035 if we aren't hiring the juniors of 2026?

THE BOARD'S ROLE: PROVIDING "COVER" Management cannot scale if the Board remains risk-averse.  Success requires "Board Cover" -the trust and strategic engagement to adapt, stop, and restart at the pace the technology now demands.

MY TAKEAWAY: AI transformation isn't just scaling software; it’s building the organizational capacity to navigate a moving target without losing judgment or nerve. This isn't about less governance; it’s about INTENTIONAL DYNAMIC GOVERNANCE - stewarding the "soul" of the company while the structure beneath it changes forever.

A heartfelt thank you to Judy LEE and Christine Fellowes for leading this event with such grace and intention. 

Grateful to my fellow WEOB members who worked tirelessly behind the scenes to manage the complexity of this event. It is a privilege to be part of this community. Anamika Talwar Sakaya Johns Rani Lisa Johnson Margaret Ma Connolly Nina Altarac  Eleonore Dachicourt.

90% of Indian AI startups won't survive the next 3 years.

Most founders blame market timing. Funding winter. Enterprise inertia.

At Quell Search, we've placed leaders across deep-tech and AI startups in the last few months. The real pattern? Hiring failures that compound silently until it's too late.

Here's what we actually see:

→ The founding team is brilliant at the technology. But they hire their next 10 people in their own image i.e all builders, no sellers.

→ By the time they realize they need a VP of Sales or a Chief Customer Officer, the product has been built for the wrong customer and the runway is 6 months.

→ They then make the worst possible hire under pressure: someone "experienced" from a large IT company who has never sold innovation, only account managed legacy contracts.

The startup dies. And the post-mortem says "enterprise adoption was slow."

No. The enterprise adoption was slow because nobody in the company knew how to earn trust with a CIO who had been burned by the last three AI vendors.

The 10% that survive have one thing in common: they hired a commercially-oriented co-founder or a GTM leader before they felt they needed one. Before the product was perfect. Before the deck was final.

Conviction hire > comfort hire. Every time.

The Indian AI ecosystem is producing genuinely world-class technology. But world-class technology doesn't sell itself. It needs world-class commercial leadership to translate potential into revenue.

At Quell Search, we exist because that gap is real, costly, and fixable.

If you're a founder reading this, when did you last make a hire that made you slightly uncomfortable because they think differently from you?

That discomfort is a signal. Chase it.

Maersk just opened a fully automated distribution centre in Singapore. Autonomous robots handle the cargo. No human hands involved.
It's a glimpse of where logistics is heading across Asia-Pacific. But three legal basics are lagging behind.

1. Possessory liens get awkward fast

A warehouse operator's lien depends on possession — physically holding the goods until you're paid. When an autonomous system is doing all the handling, and the operator never touches the cargo, the question of whether "possession" is still meaningful hasn't been properly tested. Your warehouse contract needs to address this explicitly, because the common law assumption won't save you.

2. Liability fragments across the stack

When an autonomous robot damages or loses cargo, who's responsible? The operator, the robotics manufacturer, the software vendor, the systems integrator? In Mobley v Workday (N.D. Cal. 2024), a US federal court ruled that an AI vendor could be held directly liable as an "agent" — even though the vendor didn't make the final decision. The principle matters beyond hiring: if your autonomous system makes the call, the vendor behind it may carry liability too. That's a shift.

3. AI governance is a infrastructure question, not a policy document

Every autonomous decision generates data and runs compute somewhere. The EU's revised Product Liability Directive (applicable from December 2026) now treats software — including AI — as a "product," and says a system can become defective after sale if its machine learning behaviour changes. Governance means knowing where your models run, what they ingest, and whether you can audit the decision trail. A PDF policy on a shared drive doesn't get you there.

None of this is hypothetical. These systems are already operating.

Silicon Valley has recently seen a growing trend of “acquihires” – transactions in which a company is effectively acquired for its talent, rather than its standalone business. We’re seeing these deals more frequently in the tech and AI sectors, where speed, specialized expertise, and competitive hiring pressures are reshaping how companies and investors approach M&A.

As I’ve spent more time learning about these structures, the contrast between acquihires and traditional asset deals has become increasingly clear:

▪️ In a traditional asset deal, the buyer acquires a business – its IP, contracts, goodwill, and revenue streams – through a heavily negotiated process with robust reps & warranties, indemnities, and diligence around liabilities.
▪️ In an acquihire, the “asset” is human capital. The transaction is typically structured around employment agreements, coupled with the acquisition of select assets and/or a technology license, leaving behind much of the target’s liabilities, operations, and even its core business.

The intent behind acquihires is clear: increase transaction speed, potentially reduce regulatory scrutiny (given the absence of a true “merger”), and limit risk exposure.

What’s particularly interesting is how these deals blur the line between M&A and recruiting. In many cases, they resemble a hybrid of executive hiring, IP licensing, and partial liquidity for investors, rather than a traditional acquisition.

That said, regulators are taking notice. The DOJ has recently flagged acquihires as potential antitrust avoidance mechanisms and signaled increased scrutiny. It will be interesting to see whether that pressure slows adoption, or whether this structure continues to expand into other sectors.

If you're a Procurement professional preparing for interviews at companies investing in AI, this reflects what we're hearing directly from CPOs and Procurement Leaders across Australia - and the advice we share with the permanent candidates we work with.

The distinction most people miss

Prompting ChatGPT, Claude or Gemini is not what corporates mean by AI strategy. These tools have genuine productivity value: drafting RFPs, summarising contracts, stress-testing sourcing logic. But this is individual level, not enterprise level AI.

What large organisations are actually building is a different order of magnitude.

The corporate agenda is Agentic

In 2026, the conversation in most large organisations is moving beyond curiosity. The opportunity being actively explored is specialised agents operating autonomously across procurement workflows - supplier discovery and risk monitoring, contract surveillance, sourcing and RFQ execution, spend categorisation and analytics.

This is the direction of investment and the discussion happening at ASX-listed corporates right now but real-world implementation is still at early stages, even in the most progressive environments.

The key question is where and how fast tools can be developed and implemented, although as with much of the work within Procurement, the benefits are not always clearly quantifiable.

What this means for your interview

Hiring managers aren't expecting candidates to have built agents or led transformation programs. They want to see:

- AI curiosity - a genuine interest in what these tools can do and where they are heading.
- Familiarity with LLM tools, evidenced in your personal experience with them.
- An understanding of where AI intersects with procurement workflow - sourcing, contracting, supplier management, spend analytics.
- Awareness of the governance dimensions: AI in procurement touches supplier data, commercial terms and spend decisions, all of which require human judgement and accountability - this is increasingly critical.

The differentiating answer isn't "I'm open to learning." It's being able to speak to a specific part of the procurement lifecycle where automation can create real value.

The opportunity

The people who will shape how effective AI tools are deployed in procurement are practitioners with depth of experience who build an understanding of how to use these tools to enhance their capability.

As AI absorbs an increasing share of procurement knowledge and process work, the professionals who thrive will be those who deploy their distinctly human capabilities - the judgement, experience and intuition to navigate ambiguity, the relationships that underpin commercial outcomes, and the accountability that complex decisions require.

The key theme today is Agentic, but the technology is moving quickly. The only thing anyone can reasonably predict is that in a few months' time this will likely look different again...

 For candidates?

Same rules: no fine-tuning, no RAG, no documents, no search, no domain expertise injected.

This time I asked about a policy question: how can decentralization actually help solve youth population outflow from regional cities?

---

Decentralization & Youth Outflow: What It Actually Takes

Core insight: Decentralization only works when fiscal and regulatory autonomy is designed as an integrated package — good jobs + quality of life + connectivity — backed by local capacity, fair fiscal adjustment, and performance management.

What's needed:

1) Redesign what gets decentralized
→ Not just administrative tasks, but personnel, budget, and regulatory authority as a bundled set.
→ Package youth, housing, startup, and transit powers so regions can plan holistically.

2) Align fiscal decentralization
→ Expand local tax share + performance-linked grants.
→ Incentivize outcomes (employment, net inflow); equalization support for weaker regions.

3) Regional industry & jobs
→ University-industry-government R&D + internship + hiring pipelines.
→ Region-led strategic sectors (digital, green, bio, content) with mid-tier high-wage jobs.

4) Housing & livability
→ Transit-oriented redevelopment, public rental, culture-care-healthcare bundles.
→ Set RIR (rent-to-income ratio) targets (≤25%).

5) Connectivity & mobility
→ Regional express rail, integrated fare systems, 60-min commute zones.
→ Remote/hybrid infrastructure: public coworking, gigabit internet.

6) Education & university reform
→ Contract departments with local employers, tuition/hiring packages.
→ Regional talent hiring quotas, intern tax credits.

7) Startup & regulatory sandboxes
→ Delegate sandbox authority to regions for local problem-solving startups.
→ Pre-seed matching funds, public procurement testbeds.

8) Governance
→ Mandatory youth councils + citizen jury budgeting.
→ Anchor institution consortiums: mayor + university president + employers jointly accountable.

9) Data & performance management
→ Quarterly dashboards: youth net migration, employment rate, median wage, RIR, commute time, startup survival.
→ Stage-gated execution with pivot options on failure.

10) Safeguards
→ Guardrails against subsidy wars, real estate speculation, environmental/safety standard erosion.

Execution roadmap:
